input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin is realized through a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) approach that employs SysML (including Internal Block Diagrams) as the primary modeling language. Architectural description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc are used to capture component‑connector structures, and MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines provide behavioral specifications. Development follows established methodologies like RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT to manage abstraction levels from requirements to physical implementation. Simulation and analysis are performed with MathWorks Simulink and other domain‑specific simulation tools, while executable code for active twin components is written in general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. The digital shadow reference model is expressed as a UML class diagram, serving as a basis for data aggregation and abstraction. Together, these tools, languages, and frameworks constitute the technological enablers for constructing, integrating and operating the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates multiple computational models defined through Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML (including internal block diagrams and state machines) for functional and logical architecture, and Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for component‑connector structures. Behavioural aspects are refined with MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state machines, and further detailed with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and other domain‑specific simulators. Source code artifacts (C++ or Java) may be linked to represent executable logic. Data management spans heterogeneous models across abstraction layers (requirements, functions, logics, physics) and requires integration of contextual data traces collected for a specific purpose, as defined in the digital‑shadow reference UML class diagram. Model relationships are expressed through composition, inheritance, and interface bindings within the MBSE repository, enabling traceability from physical asset data to analytical and behavioural representations.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The document outlines that DT development follows Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) with modeling split into four abstraction levels—requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer. These levels serve as representation types at successive DT lifecycle phases, and transition between phases is achieved by refining models from high‑level requirements down to detailed product models. No explicit lifecycle governance approach is described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: DT engineering is based on MBSE using SysML and architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML internal block diagrams. The development process references methodologies for handling complexity (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) and integrates simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) and code implementation (C++, Java). The document does not provide details on quality assurance, testing, version control, or evolution management strategies.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The document cites the use of standardized modeling languages—SysML (an OMG standard), ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML internal block diagrams—as the foundation for describing the Digital Twin, indicating adherence to these modeling standards. No specific industry certifications, compliance frameworks, or interoperability standards are mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
